items.content_html:
Navigation in Deep Space

In space mission, every space vehicle must continuously determine its position and velocity with sufficient accuracy to execute critical manoeuvres and meet mission objectives. Close to Earth, this navigation function is served almost effortlessly by the GNSS whose medium‑Earth‑orbit constellations broadcast time‑stamped signals that a receiver can convert into a real‑time, three‑dimensional fix accurate to a few metres. As missions extend toward the Moon, Mars, and beyond, however, this familiar capability rapidly degrades.

Signals that are robust at geocentric distances of 20 000 km turns very feeble at the lunar distance of 400 000 km, and at interplanetary ranges the received power falls so far below the thermal noise floor that no practical receiver can recover a usable measurement. Deep‑space navigation therefore relies on a hierarchy of techniques, each suited to a distinct operational regime: pushing GNSS to its physical limits with specialised lunar receivers, exploiting the exquisite radiometric precision of Earth’s Deep Space Network (DSN), and ultimately reading natural beacons - millisecond pulsars - that gives out signal pulses steadily across the galaxy. This article examines that technological side, illustrating each method with mission‑derived data, rigorous link‑budget analyses, and the fundamental equations that govern them. Figure 1 summarizes the navigation architecture adopted across different mission regimes and illustrates the transition from Earth-dependent to autonomous navigation techniques.

Fig. 1: Navigation methods across deep-space mission regimes

Limitations of GNSS Receiver

A GNSS receiver solves for its position by intersecting spheres whose radii are the pseudoranges to at least four satellites. For satellite $i$, the basic observation equation is:

$P_i = \sqrt{ (x_r - x_s^{(i)})^2 + (y_r - y_s^{(i)})^2 + (z_r - z_s^{(i)})^2 } +\ c(\delta t_r - \delta t_s^{(i)}) +\ I_i +\ T_i +\ \epsilon_i,$

where $c$ is the speed of light, $\delta t_r$ the receiver clock offset (estimated as a fourth unknown), $\delta t_s^{(i)}$ the satellite clock correction, $I_i$ and $T_i$ ionospheric and tropospheric delays, and $\epsilon_i$ noise and multipath. The formulation works brilliantly when the spacecraft is bathed in the main lobe of the GNSS antennas - but those antennas are shaped to illuminate the surface of the Earth, not deep space.

GPS L1 C/A Link Budget at Earth’s Surface

Take the GPS L1 C/A signal (1575.42 MHz, wavelength $\lambda$ $\approx$ $0.1903\ \text{m}$). The free‑space path loss over a distance $d$ is:

$ \text{FSPL(dB)} = 20\log_{10}\left( \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda} \right). $

At the GPS satellite altitude of 20,200 km (the zenith distance to a user on the ground),

$ \text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left( \frac{4\pi \times 2.02 \times 10^7}{0.1903} \right) \approx 182.5\ \text{dB}. $

Each GPS satellite transmits the L1 C/A signal with an effective isotropic radiated power (EIRP) of approximately 57 dBm (the IS‑GPS‑200 specification guarantees a minimum of 56.8 dBm for the L1 C/A signal at the edge of the Earth’s coverage). The isotropic received power at zenith is therefore:

$ P_{\text{r,iso}} = 57 - 182.5 = -125.5\ \text{dBm}. $

Real receivers use an antenna with a few dBi of gain; for a worst‑case 5° elevation angle the guaranteed minimum received power at the Earth’s surface is –128.5 dBm (–158.5 dBW). The equivalent isotropic power is $-128.5 - 3 = -131.5\ \text{dBm}$. The thermal noise power in the 2.046 MHz null‑to‑null bandwidth of the C/A code is:

$ P_n = kTB = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \times 2.046 \times 10^6 \approx -111.0\ \text{dBm}. $

Thus, before de‑spreading, the signal is buried 20.5 dB below the noise floor.

Processing gain arises from the ratio of the spread bandwidth to the data bandwidth. For GPS L1 C/A, the chip rate is 1.023 Mcps and the navigation data rate is 50 bps, so the maximum processing gain is:

$ G_{p,\text{max}} = 10\log_{10}\left( \frac{1.023 \times 10^6}{50} \right) \approx 43.1\ \text{dB}. $

A 1‑ms coherent integration uses only one full code period (1023 chips) and provides $10\log_{10}(1023) \approx 30.1\ \text{dB}$ of gain. Extending the coherent integration to 20 ms (a single data bit) recovers the full 43.1 dB, provided that the navigation data bits are known a priori, are wiped off using bit prediction, or a pilot signal is used. Applying the full processing gain to the worst‑case isotropic received power yields a post‑correlation signal‑to‑noise ratio:

$ \text{SNR}_{\text{post}} = -131.5 - (-111.0) + 43.1 \approx 22.6\ \text{dB}, $

a comfortable margin that ensures robust tracking.

Signal Degradation in Lunar Orbit

Fig. 2: GPS L1 C/A received isotropic power as a function of distance from Earth

At the Moon’s mean distance of 384 400 km, the path loss increases by

$ 20\log_{10}\left( \frac{384400}{20200} \right) \approx 25.5\ \text{dB}. $

An isotropic antenna would now see –157.0 dBm. Relative to the –111.0 dBm noise floor, the signal is 46 dB below thermal noise. Even after the 30.1 dB processing gain of a 1‑ms coherent integration, the SNR remains –15.9 dB, requiring substantial non‑coherent summation to extract. The challenge is severe, but not insurmountable: the signal is not “lost,” merely deeply hidden (this degradation is illustrated quantitatively in Figure 2, which compares received power with the thermal noise floor), and can be recovered through careful engineering.

Lunar GNSS: The LuGRE Experiment

The Lunar GNSS Receiver Experiment (LuGRE), a joint payload of NASA and the Italian Space Agency, demonstrated that GNSS signals can indeed be acquired and tracked at the Moon. Delivered to Mare Crisium by Firefly Aerospace’s Blue Ghost lander on 2 March 2025, LuGRE achieved the first GNSS position fix on the lunar surface at a distance of approximately 391 000 km from Earth. During transit it had already tracked GPS and Galileo signals at 200 000 km, shattering the previous high‑altitude record and culminating in a successful fix on the surface.

LuGRE Link Budget

Fig. 3: Comparison of GPS L1 C/A received power in representative mission scenarios

LuGRE employed a dedicated 14 dBi L‑band patch antenna pointed toward Earth. The received power at the Moon becomes

$ P_{\text{r, LuGRE}} = -157.0 + 14 = -143.0\ \text{dBm}. $

Figure 3 compares the received signal levels for terrestrial GPS reception, lunar isotropic reception, the LuGRE configuration, and Mars opposition.

The post‑correlation SNR after 20 ms coherent integration (exploiting the full 43.1 dB processing gain) is

$ \text{SNR}_{\text{20 ms}} = -143.0 - (-111.0) + 43.1 = +11.1\ \text{dB}, $

which is already comfortable. LuGRE likely achieved long coherent integrations by using Galileo’s E1C pilot signal and by performing data bit wipe‑off for the GPS L1 C/A signal, although the precise receiver implementation has not yet been described in published technical papers.

Fig. 4: Coherent integration gain for GPS L1 C/A signals

Weak‑Signal Acquisition and Tracking

The received signal after down‑conversion is modelled as

$ s(t) = A C(t-\tau) D(t) \cos\big(2\pi (f_{IF}+f_d) t + \phi\big) + n(t), $

where $C$ is the spreading code, $D$ the navigation data, $f_{IF}$ the intermediate frequency, $f_d$ the unknown Doppler shift (up to several kHz), and $n(t)$ white Gaussian noise. The receiver searches a two‑dimensional grid of code delay $\tau$ and Doppler $f_d$. For the Moon, orbital dynamics aiding – an onboard propagator that constrains the expected line‑of‑sight velocity to within a few m s⁻¹ – reduces the search space by orders of magnitude. Once acquired, the receiver tracks the carrier with a phase‑locked loop and the code with a delay‑locked loop, and generates pseudoranges for a standard least‑squares navigation solution.

LuGRE’s on‑surface fix achieved an accuracy on the order of 100 m (preliminary public data; a rigorous error analysis has yet to be published). It is worth noting that the pseudorange‑based accuracy is limited by multipath and clock errors; future lunar receivers could exploit the carrier‑phase observables, which after ambiguity resolution can deliver centimetre‑level relative positioning.

Fig. 5: Representative acquisition correlation output for a GPS signal with a pre-correlation SNR of −20 dB

But Why Lunar GNSS Cannot Scale to the Planets

Despite its success, LuGRE is fundamentally a cis‑lunar technique. The GNSS satellites remain in Earth orbit; their signals must travel from the Earth–Moon system. For a Mars‑bound vehicle at opposition (about 100 million km), the additional path loss relative to the Moon is $20\log_{10}(10^8 / 4 \times 10^5) \approx 48\ \text{dB}$, pushing the received isotropic power to roughly –205 dBm, 94 dB below the noise floor. No practical spacecraft antenna or receiver architecture can compensate for such a deficit. Deep‑space navigation must therefore rely on different physical principles.

Earth‑Based Radiometric Tracking: The Deep Space Network

For six decades, interplanetary missions have navigated using the Deep Space Network (DSN), a trio of 34‑m and 70‑m antennas in Goldstone (California), Madrid (Spain), and Canberra (Australia). The DSN provides two fundamental radiometric observables: Doppler shift for range‑rate, and round‑trip light‑time for range.

Two‑Way Doppler

A DSN station transmits a highly stable uplink carrier, typically at X‑band (7.145–7.190 GHz). The spacecraft’s transponder locks onto the carrier, multiplies its frequency by a coherent turnaround ratio (e.g. $880/749$ for X‑band), and re‑transmits the downlink. The received frequency $f_r$ at the ground station is shifted by the relative line‑of‑sight velocity $v_r$:

$ f_r = f_t \frac{880}{749} \left( 1 - \frac{2 v_r}{c} \right), $

where the factor 2 accounts for the two‑way path. Solving for $v_r$ yields the range‑rate at the epoch of reception, with a precision of better than $0.1\ \text{mm s}^{-1}$ when integrated over 60–1000 s intervals. The Juno mission, for example, exploited this sensitivity to measure Jupiter’s gravity field with exquisite accuracy.

Ranging

The DSN also measures the absolute range by sending a pseudo‑noise (PN) code or a regenerative ranging sequence. The round‑trip light time $\Delta t$ is extracted after calibrating out the spacecraft’s transponder delay $\tau_{\text{s/c}}$:

$ R = \frac{c}{2} \big( \Delta t - \tau_{\text{s/c}} \big). $

Modern regenerative ranging achieves accuracies of 1 m or better, sufficient to anchor the orbit in the radial direction. However, radiometric observables are inherently one‑dimensional: they constrain the line‑of‑sight position and velocity exquisitely, but leave the plane‑of‑sky (lateral) position almost unobservable. A single dish’s beamwidth of $\sim 0.03^\circ$ translates to a lateral uncertainty of 80 000 km at the distance of Mars. This is where interferometry becomes essential.

Delta‑DOR: Interferometric Pinpointing

Delta Differential One‑way Ranging (Delta‑DOR) transforms the DSN into a continent‑scale interferometer, providing angular measurements with nanoradian precision – equivalent to resolving a human hair from 20 000 km away.

Principles of VLBI with a Quasar Reference

Two widely separated DSN stations simultaneously record the downlink signal from the spacecraft. The difference in arrival time, $\tau_{\text{sc}}$, depends on the baseline vector $\mathbf{B}$ (the chord between the stations) and the unit direction to the spacecraft $\mathbf{\hat{s}}$:

$ \tau_{\text{sc}} =\tau_{\text{clk}} + \tau_{\text{atm}} + \tau_{\text{inst}} + \frac{\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{\hat{s}}}{c} $

The extra terms represent station‑clock offsets, tropospheric and ionospheric delays, and instrumental biases. To cancel these common‑mode errors, the observing sequence alternates between the spacecraft and a nearby quasar whose position in the International Celestial Reference Frame (ICRF) is known to better than $10^{-9}$ rad. The quasar’s delay is:

$ \tau_{\text{qso}} = \tau_{\text{clk}} + \tau_{\text{atm}} + \tau_{\text{inst}}+ \frac{\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{\hat{s}}_{\text{qso}}} {c} $

Subtracting the two yields the differential delay:

$ \Delta \tau = \tau_{\text{sc}} - \tau_{\text{qso}} \approx \frac{\mathbf{B}}{c} \cdot \big( \mathbf{\hat{s}} - \mathbf{\hat{s}}_{\text{qso}} \big). $

The angular offset from the quasar is therefore:

$ \Delta \theta \approx \frac{c \Delta \tau}{B \cos \theta}, $

where $\theta$ is the angle between the baseline and the source direction. The quasar acts as a fixed angular reference, removing all station‑based errors to first order. Residual atmospheric delays are calibrated with water‑vapour radiometers and GPS‑based ionospheric maps.

Real Performance

The DSN’s longest baselines are Goldstone–Canberra (10 560 km) and Goldstone–Madrid (8 390 km). With careful calibration, the differential delay can be measured to 30 ps (3 × 10⁻¹¹ s) under favourable observing conditions. For a baseline of 10 000 km and $\cos\theta \approx 1$, this corresponds to an angular resolution:

$ \Delta \theta = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-11}}{10^7} \approx 9 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{rad} = 0.9\ \text{nrad}. $

At the distance of Mars during the 2020 Perseverance landing (1.7 AU ≈ $2.55 \times 10^{11}$ m), a single Delta‑DOR measurement located the spacecraft to within $2.55 \times 10^{11} \times 9 \times 10^{-10} \approx 230\ \text{m}$ in the plane of the sky. In practice, operational Delta‑DOR accuracies are typically 1–5 nrad; under particularly favourable geometry, as demonstrated during the cruise of the Mars Science Laboratory, sub‑nanoradian performance (0.6 nrad) has been achieved. Juno, Cassini, and BepiColombo have all relied on the technique for precise planetary flybys.

Fig. 6: Delta-DOR angular resolution

Limitations of Delta‑DOR

Delta‑DOR is not an autonomous method. It requires simultaneous visibility of the spacecraft and a bright, compact quasar from at least two DSN complexes, which imposes scheduling constraints. Solar plasma scintillation can degrade the measurement when the line of sight passes close to the Sun. Most importantly, the technique ties the mission to Earth‑bound infrastructure: the position solution is not available onboard in real time, and a round‑trip command cycle for a spacecraft at Jupiter exceeds 1.5 hours.

Optical Navigation: Autonomous Angular Measurements

For missions requiring onboard autonomy – or operating on the far side of the Sun – optical navigation (OpNav) provides a self‑contained alternative. A star tracker or dedicated navigation camera images a target body (planet, moon, or asteroid) against a background of reference stars. The centroid of the illuminated body is determined to a fraction of a pixel, yielding an angular measurement. If the pixel field‑of‑view (IFOV) is $20\ \mu\text{rad}$ and the centroiding accuracy is $0.1$ pixel, the angular uncertainty is $2\ \mu\text{rad}$. At a range of $10^5$ km, the linear position error in the sky plane is $200\ \text{m}$.

The basic observation equation is:

$ \mathbf{b}_{\text{obs}} = \mathbf{T} \left( \frac{\mathbf{r}_{T}-\mathbf{r}_{\text{s/c}}} {\left|\mathbf{r}_{T}-\mathbf{r}_{\text{s/c}}\right|}\right)+\boldsymbol{\nu} $

where $\mathbf{r}_{T}$ is the target body’s position relative to the Solar System barycentre, $\mathbf{r}_{\text{s/c}}$ is the spacecraft position, $\mathbf{T}$ the camera orientation matrix, and $\boldsymbol{\nu}$ measurement noise. By imaging the same target from different positions along the orbit, the spacecraft can triangulate its trajectory.

Historically, OpNav images were processed on Earth – the Voyager spacecraft, for example, relied on ground‑based reduction of its optical observations to refine trajectories at Jupiter and Saturn. Truly autonomous optical navigation was demonstrated by Deep Space 1; its AutoNav system processed optical images and combined them with an onboard dynamical model to propagate the spacecraft state in real time, forming a complete autonomous navigation loop. Since then, fully autonomous OpNav has been employed by OSIRIS‑REx (which navigated to within a few hundred metres of Bennu without ground intervention) and by DART during its terminal approach to Dimorphos. These missions show that OpNav can serve as the backbone of real‑time, self‑contained navigation near a target body.

XNAV: Pulsar‑Based Navigation

To break free of all Earth and Solar‑system references, researchers have turned to the most stable rotators known: millisecond pulsars. X‑ray pulsar‑based navigation (XNAV) treats these rapidly spinning neutron stars as a naturally occurring, galaxy‑wide positioning system.

Pulsar Timing Model

At the Solar System Barycentre (SSB), the rotational phase of a pulsar can be described by a Taylor series:

$ \Phi_{\text{SSB}}(t) = \Phi_0 + \nu (t - t_0) + \frac{1}{2} \dot{\nu} (t - t_0)^2 + \cdots, $

where $\nu$ is the spin frequency and $\dot{\nu}$ the secular slow‑down rate (typically $10^{-15}$–$10^{-18}$ s⁻²). The phases are so predictable that the SSB arrival time of the N‑th pulse can be predicted years in advance with microsecond accuracy.

A spacecraft carries an X‑ray telescope that records photon arrival times. After folding the photons modulo the pulsar period, a pulse profile is obtained and cross‑correlated with a high‑signal‑to‑noise template to yield a pulse time‑of‑arrival (TOA) at the spacecraft’s proper time $t_{\text{obs}}$. The geometric relationship is

$ t_{\text{SSB}} = t_{\text{obs}} + \frac{\mathbf{\hat{n}} \cdot \mathbf{r}}{c} + \Delta_{\text{rel}} + \Delta_{\text{clock}}, $

where $\mathbf{\hat{n}}$ is the precisely known unit vector to the pulsar, $\mathbf{r}$ the spacecraft position vector relative to the SSB, $\Delta_{\text{rel}}$ the relativistic Einstein and Shapiro delays, and $\Delta_{\text{clock}}$ the offset from a stable onboard timescale. The dominant term, $(\mathbf{\hat{n}} \cdot \mathbf{r})/c$, is the Rømer delay – the light‑travel time across the projection of the spacecraft’s position onto the pulsar line‑of‑sight.

Measurement Precision

The fundamental limit on TOA accuracy for a pulsar with a simple Gaussian‑like pulse of width $W$ is

$ \sigma_{\text{TOA}} \approx \frac{W}{\text{SNR}}, \qquad \text{SNR} \approx \frac{S_p \sqrt{T}}{\sqrt{S_p + S_{\text{bg}}}}, $

where $S_p$ is the pulsed photon count rate, $S_{\text{bg}}$ the combined background rate (diffuse X‑ray sky, detector internal background), and $T$ the integration time.

A concrete, order‑of‑magnitude estimate illuminates the scale: PSR B1937+21, the first millisecond pulsar, has a period of 1.56 ms, a pulsed X‑ray flux of $\sim 2 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{erg cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}$ in the 0.5–10 keV band, and a pulse width of about 10 % of the period ($W \approx 0.156\ \text{ms}$). With an effective area of $2000\ \text{cm}^2$ (representative of NICER’s 56 X‑ray mirrors), the detected pulsed count rate is

$ S_p \approx \frac{2 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{erg cm}^{-2}\text{s}^{-1}}{1.6 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{erg keV}^{-1} \times 1.5\ \text{keV}} \times 2000\ \text{cm}^2 \approx 0.17\ \text{ct s}^{-1}. $

The background (cosmic X‑ray plus internal) is typically $S_{\text{bg}} \approx 1.0\ \text{ct s}^{-1}$. Over an 8‑hour integration ($T = 28800\ \text{s}$), the pulsed counts are $0.17 \times 28800 \approx 4900$ and background counts $\approx 28800$. The SNR is

$ \text{SNR} \approx \frac{4900}{\sqrt{4900 + 28800}} \approx 26. $

The TOA uncertainty then is

$ \sigma_{\text{TOA}} \approx \frac{0.156 \times 10^{-3}}{26} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 6\ \mu\text{s}. $

Multiplying by $c$ gives a line‑of‑sight position error of about 1.8 km. With three or more non‑coplanar pulsars, the 3‑D position can be solved to a level of a few kilometres.

Fig. 7: Expected XNAV performance for PSR B1937+21.

SEXTANT Demonstration

The Station Explorer for X‑ray Timing and Navigation Technology (SEXTANT) aboard the Neutron Star Interior Composition Explorer (NICER) on the International Space Station proved the concept in 2017–2018. Using a carefully selected catalogue of millisecond pulsars (including PSR J0030+0451, J0218+4232, B1937+21, and B1821‑24), SEXTANT achieved fully autonomous, real‑time 3‑D position fixes with an accuracy of 10 km after 8 hours of observation, and 5 km after 2 days. This was the first demonstration that a spacecraft could determine its absolute position in the Solar System without any contact with Earth.

Challenges with XNAV

XNAV is not yet turn‑key for all missions. The major obstacles include:

  • Detector mass and power: A square‑metre‑class X‑ray telescope is heavy and consumes significant power, competing with scientific instruments.
  • Onboard clock stability: An atomic clock is essential; an error of 1 ns maps to 0.3 m of range. The Deep Space Atomic Clock (DSAC), a mercury‑ion trap demonstrated in 2019, exhibits a fractional frequency stability of $<10^{-15}$ over days, corresponding to a time error of less than 1 µs after several days.
  • Pulsar timing noise: Glitches, red noise, and instabilities in the interstellar medium degrade the SSB prediction over time.
  • Integration time: A quick fix still requires several hours of photon collection, which may conflict with mission operations.
  • Sky distribution: Pulsars are weak X‑ray sources, and the brightest usable ones are unevenly scattered on the sky, limiting the instantaneous navigation geometry and requiring careful catalogue selection.

Despite these challenges, XNAV remains the only conceivable method for fully autonomous navigation across the entire Solar System – and perhaps beyond.

Onboard State Estimation

In practice, none of the techniques described above operates in isolation. Every deep‑space spacecraft continuously propagates its state using an onboard dynamical model and, typically, an inertial measurement unit (IMU) or gyroscopes. External measurements – whether radiometric, optical, or pulsar‑based – are periodically fused with this dead‑reckoned state through a navigation filter, almost always an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) or Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF).

The filter maintains a state vector $\mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{r}, \mathbf{v}, \delta t_{\text{clk}}, \dots]^T$ and a covariance matrix $\mathbf{P}$. Between measurements, the state is propagated using a high‑fidelity force model:

$ \dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t) + \mathbf{w}, $

where $\mathbf{f}$ accounts for solar radiation pressure, third‑body perturbations, and thruster firings, and $\mathbf{w}$ is process noise. When an observation $z$ arrives (a pseudorange, a Doppler shift, a quasar‑differential delay, an optical centroid, or a pulsar TOA), the filter computes the innovation $\mathbf{y} = z - h(\mathbf{x}^-)$, the measurement Jacobian $\mathbf{H}$, and updates the state and covariance:

$ \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^T (\mathbf{H} \mathbf{P}^- \mathbf{H}^T + \mathbf{R})^{-1}, $ $ \mathbf{x}^+ = \mathbf{x}^- + \mathbf{K} \mathbf{y}, $ $ \mathbf{P}^+ = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K} \mathbf{H}) \mathbf{P}^-. $

This continuous fusion of measurements with a dynamical model is what turns a few sparse observations into a coherent, high‑precision orbit solution. Without it, a spacecraft would drift into useless uncertainty within hours. The filter also provides the crucial real‑time covariance that tells the onboard computer – or the ground team – whether the navigation solution is good enough for the next critical manoeuvre.

Most Probable Solution

No single technique rules deep space. Future missions will fuse multiple sources in an onboard Kalman filter, as outlined above. The emerging architecture can be summarised as:

Domain Technique Observable Accuracy
Near‑Earth (LEO‑Moon) GNSS (LuGRE‑enhanced) Pseudorange, carrier phase ~100 m (pseudorange); sub‑metre to cm possible with future carrier‑phase ambiguity resolution
Interplanetary cruise DSN Doppler + Ranging + Delta‑DOR Range, range‑rate, plane‑of‑sky angle 1 m radial, 150–300 m lateral @ 1 AU
Approach and rendezvous OpNav (optical navigation) Angular separation from target 100 m @ $10^5$ km
Deep‑space autonomous XNAV (pulsar TOA) + OpNav + inertial propagation Absolute 3‑D position 5–10 km (hours)
Timekeeping Deep Space Atomic Clock Onboard time $<1\ \mu\text{s}$ error over days

In this vision, Earth‑based tracking provides the “gold standard” for cruise, OpNav takes over when real‑time autonomy is needed near a target, and XNAV fills the gap when the Earth is occulted or too far for timely updates. The LuGRE experiment has already shattered the myth that GNSS cannot work at the Moon, opening the door to a future where lunar orbiters and landers navigate autonomously with metre‑level accuracy. Delta‑DOR, refined over decades, remains the workhorse for planetary flybys. And pulsar navigation, having moved from theory to flight demonstration, points the way to a truly self‑sufficient spacecraft.

Taken together, the techniques described here, from extended‑sensitivity GNSS at the Moon, through Earth‑based radiometric tracking and interferometry, to autonomous optical and pulsar‑based navigation, form the practical toolkit of the deep‑space navigator. Each method addresses a specific range and autonomy requirement, and all are routinely combined within onboard estimation filters to produce the continuous, reliable trajectory knowledge on which every mission depends.

items.title: The Real-World Guide to CubeSat Sensor & Actuator Selection
items.summary: This isn’t a lecture on control theory. This is a guide on how to buy the hardware that will keep your satellite pointing at the ground (or the stars) rather than tumbling aimlessly in Low Earth Orbit (LEO). Before you open a catalog, you need…
items.content_html:
If you are reading this, you are likely staring at a blank spreadsheet labeled "ADCS Budget" and realizing that the textbooks didn't tell you which part number to buy. I’ve been there. I’ve seen beautiful control laws fail because the sensor noise was higher than the datasheet promised, and I’ve seen missions lost because someone underestimated the magnetic dipole of their own battery cables.

This isn’t a lecture on control theory. This is a guide on how to buy the hardware that will keep your satellite pointing at the ground (or the stars) rather than tumbling aimlessly in Low Earth Orbit (LEO).

1. The Design Requirements: What Do You Actually Need?

Before you open a catalog, you need to lock down your requirements. "Pointing as good as possible" is not a requirement; it’s a recipe for bankruptcy.

In my experience, 90% of CubeSat missions fall into one of these three tiers. Decide which one you are right now.

Tier A: The "Just Survive" (Nadir Pointing / Power Positive)

  • Goal: Keep the solar panels roughly facing the sun and the antenna roughly facing Earth.
  • Accuracy: \(\pm 5^{\circ}\) to \(\pm 10^{\circ}\).
  • Slew Rate: Low (\(< 1^{\circ}/s\)).
  • Typical Payload: Basic comms relay, simple science experiments.

Tier B: The "Look at This City" (Medium Precision)

  • Goal: Take a photo of a specific city or track a large ground feature.
  • Accuracy: \(\pm 0.5^{\circ}\) to \(\pm 2^{\circ}\).
  • Slew Rate: Moderate. You need to slew to targets quickly to maximize ground station time.
  • Typical Payload: Medium-res Earth observation, spectral imagers.

Tier C: The "Pinpoint" (High Precision)

  • Goal: Laser communications, astronomy, or sub-meter ground resolution.
  • Accuracy: \(< 0.1^{\circ}\) (often \(< 0.01^{\circ}\) pointing stability).
  • Slew Rate: High agility or extreme stability.
  • Typical Payload: Star trackers are mandatory here.

2. Numerical Analysis & Sizing (The Physics)

Let’s simulate a design cycle. We will size a 3U CubeSat (approx. \(10 \times 10 \times 34\) cm, 4 kg mass) in a 500 km SSO (Sun-Synchronous Orbit).

You cannot select an actuator until you know your Disturbance Torques. These are the invisible hands trying to spin your satellite.

Step 1: Calculate Your Inertia Matrix

For a 3U CubeSat, we approximate it as a solid rectangular prism.

  • \(Mass (m) = 4.0 \text{ kg}\)
  • \(Dimensions = 0.1 \text{ m} \times 0.1 \text{ m} \times 0.34 \text{ m}\)

$$I_{xx} = I_{yy} = \frac{m}{12} (0.1^2 + 0.34^2) = 0.0418 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$$

$$I_{zz} \text{ (Long axis)} = \frac{m}{12} (0.1^2 + 0.1^2) = 0.0067 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m}^2$$

Note: In reality, your deployable solar panels will change this. Always add a 20% margin to your inertia estimates early in the project.

Step 2: Calculate Disturbance Torques (Worst Case)

We need to know the maximum torque the environment will throw at us.

A. Gravity Gradient Torque (\(T_g\))

This is the Earth trying to pull your long axis towards the center of the planet.

$$T_g = \frac{3\mu}{2R^3} |I_z - I_y| \sin(2\theta)$$

  • \(\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^2\)
  • \(R = 6371+500 \text{ km} = 6871 \text{ km}\)
  • Max deviation \(\theta = 45^{\circ}\) (worst case)

$$T_g \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \times (0.0418 - 0.0067) \times 1 \approx \mathbf{5.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Nm}}$$

Verdict: Negligible for active control, but useful for passive stabilization.

B. Aerodynamic Torque (\(T_a\))

(A big problem in LEO)

$$T_a = 0.5 \rho V^2 C_d A (cp - cm)$$

  • \(\rho \text{ (Density at 500km)} \approx 2 \times 10^{-13} \text{ kg/m}^3\) (varies wildly with solar cycle!)
  • \(V = 7600 \text{ m/s}\)
  • \(C_d = 2.2\) (Drag coefficient)
  • \(A = 0.034 \text{ m}^2\) (Long side facing velocity)
  • \((cp - cm)\) (Offset between center of pressure and mass) \(= 0.05 \text{ m}\) (Assume 5 cm offset)

$$T_a = 0.5 (2\times 10^{-13})(7600^2)(2.2)(0.034)(0.05) \approx \mathbf{2.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Nm}}$$

Verdict: This is your baseline. Your wheels need to hold this constantly.

C. Magnetic Disturbance (\(T_m\))

If your satellite has a residual dipole (from batteries, current loops), Earth's field grabs it.

$$T_m = D \times B$$

  • \(D = 0.1 \text{ Am}^2\) (Typical poor wiring hygiene in CubeSats)
  • \(B = 45,000 \text{ nT} = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}\)

$$T_m = 0.1 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5} = \mathbf{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Nm}}$$

Step 3: Sizing the Actuators

Torque Requirement:
To stay stable, your Reaction Wheels (RW) must generate significantly more torque than the disturbances. Total Disturbance \(\approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Nm}\). However, you also need Agility (Slew Rate). To rotate \(90^{\circ}\) in 60 seconds:

$$\alpha = \frac{2\theta}{t^2} = \frac{2(\pi/2)}{60^2} \approx 0.00087 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

$$T_{slew} = I \alpha = 0.0418 \times 0.00087 \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Nm}$$

Total Required Torque: \(\approx 0.1 \text{ mNm}\) (safe margin).

Momentum Saturation (The Trap):
Aerodynamic torque is constant in one direction for half the orbit. It builds up momentum in the wheels.

$$h_{build} = T_{aero} \times \text{Time} = 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times (3000 \text{ sec}) \approx \mathbf{63 \text{ mNms}}$$

Crucial: If your wheel only holds 30 mNms, it will saturate (reach max speed) halfway through the orbit, and you will lose control. You need Magnetorquers to "dump" this momentum.

3. The Hardware: A Market Overview

Now that we have the numbers (\(0.1 \text{ mNm}\) torque, \(>60 \text{ mNms}\) momentum), let's look at what you can actually buy.

Actuators

1. Reaction Wheels (RW)
  • A Fair Choice: Sinclair Interplanetary (now Rocket Lab) RW-0.03
    • Stats: 30-40 mNms momentum, 2 mNm torque.
    • My Take: The industry workhorse. Extremely reliable. However, for our 3U calculation above, 30 mNms is too small if drag is high. You would need to dump momentum constantly.
  • The Heavy Lifter: CubeSpace Gen2 Small/Medium
    • Stats: Ranges from 17 mNms to ~100+ mNms.
    • My Take: For a 3U in 500km orbit, I’d look at a wheel offering at least 50-80 mNms. CubeSpace wheels are robust and integrate well with their own ADCS stacks.
  • GomSpace NanoTorque
    • Stats: Integrated 4-wheel pyramid units.
    • My Take: Great for volume saving (compact), but fixing a single failed wheel inside a pyramid block is impossible.
2. Magnetorquers (MTQ)

You must have these to desaturate your wheels.

  • Torque Rods: Usually fit inside the rails of the chassis.
    • Requirement: We calculated a magnetic disturbance of \(4.5 \mu\text{Nm}\). To fight this and dump momentum, you need a dipole of at least \(0.2 \text{ Am}^2\).
  • Options: ISISpace, GomSpace, and CubeSpace all offer rods. Pro tip: Check the "Residual Dipole" of the rod itself. A magnetized core can ruin your magnetometer readings.

Sensors

1. Sun Sensors (Coarse vs. Fine)
  • Coarse (Photodiodes): Every CubeSat needs 6 of these (one on each face). They tell you "Sun is generally that way."
    • Cost: <$100 (if you build them).
  • Fine Sun Sensors:
    • Option: CubeSpace CubeSense or Lens R&D BiSon64.
    • Specs: \(<1^{\circ}\) accuracy, radiation hardened.
    • Use Case: If you don't have a star tracker, this is your primary reference vector.
2. Star Trackers (Precision: If you neeed it!)

If you need \(<0.1^{\circ}\) pointing (Tier C), stop looking at sun sensors and buy this.

  • Blue Canyon Nano Star Tracker (NST)
    • Specs: ~4 arcseconds accuracy.
    • Experience: The benchmark for high-performance missions.
  • CubeSpace CubeStar
    • Specs: Small form factor, \(\sim 0.02^{\circ}\) accuracy.
    • Experience: A solid performer for 3U buses where volume is tight.
3. Magnetometers
  • Usually integrated into the ADCS computer.
  • Warning: Place this as far from your battery and reaction wheels as possible. I once saw a mission where the magnetometer just measured the reaction wheel spinning up. We had to use the torque commands to filter the magnetic data. Don't be that person. Deployable booms are worth the mechanical risk.

4. The Go-To Guide: Decision Matrix

Use this flowchart logic to select your suite.

Step A: Actuators Selection

Your Mission Profile Recommended Configuration Component Sizing (3U Example)
Simple Tumbling/Detumble Magnetorquers Only 3x Torque Rods (\(>0.2 \text{ Am}^2\))
Earth Pointing (Loose) 3 Reaction Wheels + MTQs Wheels: \(>10 \text{ mNms}\) storage
High Agility / Precision 4 Reaction Wheels (Pyramid) Wheels: \(>50 \text{ mNms}\) storage (Redundancy + Torque)

Step B: Sensor Selection

Pointing Accuracy Req Required Sensor Suite
\(> 5^{\circ}\) Magnetometer + Coarse Sun Diodes
\(1^{\circ} - 5^{\circ}\) Magnetometer + Fine Sun Sensor + Gyro (IMU)
\(< 0.1^{\circ}\) Star Tracker + Gyro + Sun Sensors (for safe mode)

5. Some Engineering Advice 

  1. The 'KRAKsat' Loop: You might think power and ADCS are different subsystems, but they are deeply coupled. The KRAKsat mission failed because it entered a tumble. The tumble meant the solar panels couldn't point at the sun, causing voltage to drop. The satellite would die, recharge slightly, boot up, and immediately try to detumble—draining the battery before it could stabilize. It entered a reboot loop (150 times per orbit!) until it died. Lesson: Your ADCS needs a passive "zero-power" or "low-power" mode that allows the batteries to charge before you fire up the torque rods.
  2. The "Lost in Space" Problem: Star trackers are amazing, but they can be blinded by the Sun or Moon. If your Star Tracker is blinded and you don't have Sun sensors as a backup, your satellite is effectively blind. Always have a "Safe Mode" sensor (Sun sensors + Magnetometer).
  3. Wheel Jitter: Big reaction wheels store more momentum, but they vibrate more. If you have a sensitive camera, a poorly balanced wheel will blur your images. Look for wheels with "micro-vibration" specs.
  4. Hardware-in-the-Loop (HITL): Do not launch code you haven't tested on the real hardware. Simulate the orbit, feed the sensors fake data, and watch the wheels spin on your desk. If it acts weird on the desk, it will act weird in space.

Professional AOCS Design Services

If you are reading this and realizing that your mission profile is more complex than a standard spreadsheet can handle, I can help. I offer professional AOCS design and consultation services tailored to your specific payload requirements.

Services Offered:

  • Architecture & Trade Studies: I will help you select the exact sensor/actuator suite that meets your pointing requirements without breaking your power or mass budget.
  • Control Loop Design: Custom PID, LQR, or Kalman Filter design for non-standard operational modes (e.g., target tracking, formation flying).
  • Simulation & Analysis: Full Monte Carlo analysis of your disturbance environment to prove to stakeholders that your satellite will not tumble.
  • Hardware-in-the-Loop Planning: I can design the test campaign to verify your ADCS hardware before it gets integrated.

Contact me to discuss how we can ensure your satellite points exactly where it needs to, every time. 

PS: You can play with the beta version of the simplified AOCS budgeting software tool I developed here.

items.title: A Simple Guide to Design Robust Attitude and Orbit Controller for Satellites
items.summary: My usual approach to learn a new system involves learning about its history and how it came into existence. Today we are exploring the robust controllers used in the satellites. Early satellites from the 1950s and 60s, such as Explorer 1 and Vanguard, relied on…
items.content_html:
My usual approach to learn a new system involves learning about its history and how it came into existence. Today we are exploring the robust controllers used in the satellites.

Some Interesting History

Early satellites from the 1950s and 60s, such as Explorer 1 and Vanguard, relied on simple attitude control methods to keep the spacecraft stable and correctly aligned which was done often using passive stabilization through spin mechanics or basic magnetic torquers. These methods worked well for the lightweight spacecraft of that era, but as missions grew more complex, the need for better control systems increased.

Coming to the 1970s and 80s, we had scientific missions like Skylab, Viking, and Voyager needed precise pointing for imaging, communication, and navigation. This turned attention to closed-loop feedback control, which used reaction wheels, star trackers, and gyroscopes. Conventional control methods, such as PID loops, decoupled axis controllers, and gain-scheduled linear controllers, became standard in spacecraft Attitude and Orbit Control Systems (AOCS). These controllers worked well under normal conditions, but they had a significant drawback. As missions became more complex, spacecraft dynamics revealed strong couplings, flexible vibrations, actuator nonlinearities, and major uncertainties. Traditional controllers struggled to ensure stability or performance when the spacecraft deviated from established models.

By the 1980s, the limits of traditional control theory were clear with the rise of large flexible spacecraft, deployable structures, and highly accurate Earth-observing satellite constellations. This led to a focus on control methods aimed at not just improving performance but also maintaining stability in light of modeling errors, disturbances, and worst-case scenarios. Organizations like NASA and ESA began to test these methods on projects such as the Hubble Space Telescope, ENVISAT, and various technology demonstrations.

Leading this change was H∞ (H-infinity) control, a concept that rapidly evolved throughout the 1990s. Unlike controllers based on exact models, H∞ includes structured uncertainty, disturbance management, and worst-case scenario handling directly in the design. This approach is particularly useful for satellites facing environmental torques, shifting mass properties due to fuel burnoff, flexible solar arrays, or issues with reaction wheels. In the following decades, H∞ control, along with robust methods like μ-synthesis LQG/LTR and gain-scheduling with robustness margins, became critical elements in advanced AOCS design.

Today, as spacecraft grow larger, more agile, and increasingly autonomous—from mega-constellation satellites to interplanetary probes—it is required to make the AOCS robust to various operational conditions. They form the basis of AOCS design, allowing satellites to maintain stability and functionality amid the many uncertainties found in the challenging space environment.

In the following blog, I will show how H∞ control can be applied to a typical AOCS problem, demonstrating its ability to provide reliable closed-loop performance even with considerable model uncertainties and external disturbances.

Some Mathematics

Here is some mathematical background that will come in handy.

System Norms

  • $H_2$ Norm: Root mean square value of the output for white noise input.
  • $H_∞$ Norm: Worst-case energy amplification from input to output.

$$ ||G(s)||_∞ = max_ω σ̄(G(jω)) $$ where $σ̄ $ is the maximum singular value.

The Small Gain Theorem

A feedback system stays stable if:
$$ ||Δ(s)||_∞ · ||T(s)||_∞ < 1 $$ where $Δ$ represents uncertainty and $T$ is the complementary sensitivity.

Mixed Sensitivity Problem

The standard $H_∞$ problem minimizes:
$$ || \begin{matrix} W_p S ,\ W_u K S ,\ W_T T \end{matrix} ||_∞ $$

where:

$S$ = Sensitivity function $(I + GK)^{-1}$

$T$ = Complementary sensitivity $I - S$

$W_p$ = Performance weight

$W_u$ = Control effort weight

$W_T$ = Robustness weight

A Little Context

Let’s design an Attitude and Orbit Control System (AOCS) for a large, flexible communication satellite (it has kind of large solar arrays!) in Geostationary Orbit (GEO). This application suits H∞ well due to the complex and high-fidelity dynamics. In general, there are various modes of control in the AOCS for satellite, and generally, the kind of mode depends on what the actual objective of the particular mode.

Step 1: Mathematical Modeling

Satellite Rigid Body Dynamics

Let’s keep it simple and we have the usual torque-inertia equation: $$ Jω̇ + ω × Jω = τ_{ctrl} + τ_{dist} $$

where:

  • $J$ = Inertia tensor
  • $ω$ = Angular velocity vector
  • $τ_{ctrl}$ = Control torque
  • $τ_{dist}$ = Disturbance torque
Flexible Mode Dynamics

The solar arrays are not rigid. They have bending and twisting modes. This is modeled by adding modal coordinates. Let’s consider $N$ flexible modes: $$ [J, \Gamma; \Gamma^T, I ] [\dot\omega, \ddot\eta ]+… = [\tau_{ctrl};0 ] $$ where:

  • $η$ = Flexible mode coordinates
  • $Γ$ = Rigid-flex coupling matrix (coupling between rigid and flexible motion)
  • $Ω$ = Flexible mode frequency matrix
  • $ζ$ = Damping ratio matrix
Actuator Dynamics

Here, for this example, let’s consider we have reaction wheen as the only actuator. Reaction wheels have a maximum torque/speed limit and are modeled with a first-order lag: $$\frac{1}{0.02s+1} $$

Sensor Dynamics

Star trackers and gyros have noise and delays. The star tracker might have a low-pass characteristic and a processing delay of more or less 100ms.

Step 2: Control Objectives and Uncertainty

  • High-Performance Pointing: Track a commanded attitude (for example, from a ground station) with an error of less than 0.001° (RMS).
  • Vibration Suppression: Actively dampen the vibrations of the solar arrays to avoid blurring of onboard optical payloads.
  • Manage Control Effort: Do not overwork the reaction wheels.
  • Robustness: Keep stability and performance despite:
  • Parametric Uncertainty: Mass properties (J) change as fuel is used. Payloads can be deployed, which alters the inertia.
    Mass uncertainty: $$ m_{real} = m_{nom} × (1 + δ_m), $$ where, $ |δ_m| ≤ 0.2 $ Flexible mode frequency uncertainty: $$ ω_{real} = ω_{nom} × (1 + δ_ω), $$ where, $ |δ_ω| ≤ 0.3 $
  • Dynamic Uncertainty: The exact frequency and damping of the flexible modes are unknown and can vary with temperature. We might only know that a mode falls between 0.8 Hz and 1.2 Hz.
    Multiplicative uncertainty representation: $$ G_{real}(s) = G_{nom}(s)(1 + W_Δ(s)Δ(s)) $$ where, $||Δ(s)||_∞ ≤ 1$
  • Unmodeled Dynamics: There are higher-order flexible modes beyond the six we modeled.

I can tabulate below them as well:

Objective Mathematical Representation Physical Meaning
Precise Pointing Minimize tracking error Maintain payload alignment
Disturbance Rejection Minimize $||S||_∞$ Resist environmental torques
Control Effort Management Minimize $||KS||_∞$ Prevent actuator saturation
Robust Stability Minimize $||T||_∞$ Handle model uncertainties
Flexible Mode Damping Shape loop gain around flexible frequencies Suppress solar array vibrations

Step 3: Setting up the H∞ Control Design

Let’s formulate this as mixed sensitivity H∞ problem, where we are required to define the generalized plant with weights. Well, the performance, control and uncertainty weights are described below.

  • Performance Weight ($W_p$): It is based on the tracking error, $e = θ_{ref} - θ$. It is high at low frequencies to ensure good tracking and decreases at high frequencies.
  • Control Effort Weight ($W_u$): This is applied to the control signal u (wheel commands). It is low at low frequencies to permit control action and increases at high frequencies to stop the controller from triggering high-frequency unmodeled dynamics.
  • Uncertainty Weight ($W_Δ$): This is used to handle the flexible mode uncertainty. We apply a high-pass filter shape that is small at low frequencies, where our model performs well, and large at high frequencies, where the model does not perform as well. And, finally as described above the controller gain $K(s)$ is synthesized by minimizing: $$ || \begin{matrix} W_p S ,\ W_u K S ,\ W_T T \end{matrix} ||_∞ $$

Minimizing this norm directly balances performance, control effort, and robustness.

Controller Design Process

State-Space Representation

The system can be represented in the state-space form as:

% System matrices for n flexible modes
A = [0         I         0         0;
     -K_rigid  -D_rigid  -K_coup   0;
     0         0         0         I;
     -K_coup'  0         -K_flex   -D_flex];
     
B = [0; I; 0; gamma'];
C = [I 0 0 0];  % Attitude measurement

Step 1: System Identification

Parameter Estimation

% Nominal parameters
J_nom = diag([1000, 800, 1200]);  % kg·m²
freq_modes = [0.8, 2.5, 5.0];     % Hz
damping_ratio = 0.01;             % 1% damping

Model Construction

% Build state-space model with flexible modes
n_states = 2 + 2 * length(freq_modes);
[A, B, C, D] = buildFlexibleSatelliteModel(J_nom, freq_modes, damping_ratio);
sat_plant = ss(A, B, C, D);

Step 2: Weight Selection

Performance Weight (W_p)
s = tf('s');
W_perf = 0.1 * (s/0.1 + 1)/(s/10 + 1);

Recall, characteristics:

  • High gain at low frequencies for good tracking
  • Roll-off at high frequencies to avoid high-frequency noise amplification
Control Effort Weight (W_u)
W_control = 0.01 * (s/5 + 1)/(s/0.5 + 1);

Recall, purpose: Penalize large control inputs to prevent actuator saturation

Robustness Weight (W_T)
W_robust = 0.3 * (s/1 + 1)/(s/10 + 1);

Recall, role: Shape complementary sensitivity for robustness to high-frequency uncertainties

Step 3: Generalized Plant Construction

systemnames = 'P W_perf W_control W_robust';
inputvar = '[w; u]';
outputvar = '[W_perf; W_control; W_robust; e]';
input_to_P = '[u + w]';
input_to_W_perf = '[e]';
input_to_W_control = '[u]';
input_to_W_robust = '[P]';

P_gen = sysic;

Step 4: H-Infinity Synthesis

[K, ~, gamma] = hinfsyn(P_gen, nmeas, ncont);

Algorithm Steps:

  1. Solve two Riccati equations
  2. Check existence conditions
  3. Construct controller state-space matrices
  4. Verify achieved performance level $γ$

Step 5: Controller Reduction

if order(K) > 8
    K_red = reduce(K, 8);  % Reduce to manageable order
end

Implementation in MATLAB

Complete Design Code Structure

%% AOCS H-Infinity Design Pipeline
% 1. System Modeling
[A, B, C, D] = buildSatelliteModel(parameters);
P = ss(A, B, C, D);

% 2. Weight Selection  
[W_perf, W_control, W_robust] = designWeights(requirements);

% 3. Generalized Plant
P_gen = buildGeneralizedPlant(P, W_perf, W_control, W_robust);

% 4. Controller Synthesis
[K, gamma] = synthesizeHinfController(P_gen);

% 5. Validation
[pass, margins] = validateDesign(P, K, requirements);

Stability Margin Enforcement

function [K_optimal, design_info] = synthesizeAOCSController(P_plant, requirements, max_iterations)
    % Iterative synthesis with margin enforcement
    for iter = 1:max_iterations
        % H-infinity synthesis
        [K, ~, gamma] = hinfsyn(P_gen, 1, 1);
        
        % Check margins against requirements
        [pass, margins] = checkStabilityMargins(P_plant*K, requirements);
        
        if pass && margins.meets_target
            break;  % Requirements satisfied
        else
            % Adjust weights and iterate
            [W_perf, W_control] = adjustWeights(margins, requirements);
        end
    end
end

Performance Analysis

Stability Margins Analysis

Industry Standards

When I say industry standards, I mean the “typical” values being used at ISRO (from my experience), at NASA and ESA (from their respective reference documents). What stability margins will fit to your needs depends on your objective.

Mission Type Gain Margin Phase Margin Flexible Mode GM Flexible Mode PM
Conservative ≥ 8 dB ≥ 45° ≥ 6 dB ≥ 30°
Typical ≥ 6 dB ≥ 35° ≥ 4 dB ≥ 25°
Aggressive ≥ 4 dB ≥ 25° ≥ 3 dB ≥ 20°
Human-Rated ≥ 12 dB ≥ 60° ≥ 9 dB ≥ 45°

Margin Calculation

function [pass, margin_info] = checkStabilityMargins(L, requirements)
    [GM, PM, Wcg, Wcp] = margin(L);
    GM_db = 20*log10(GM);
    
    % Check against requirements
    pass_GM = GM_db >= requirements.GM_min;
    pass_PM = PM >= requirements.PM_min;
    
    % Flexible mode margins
    for i = 1:length(flexible_freqs)
        [mag, phase] = bode(L, 2*pi*flexible_freqs(i));
        % Calculate effective margins at each flexible mode
    end
end

Time Domain Performance

Step Response Specifications

Again, these are “typical” values, and as per your objective, they can vary:

  • Rise Time: < 30 seconds for large maneuvers
  • Settling Time: < 60 seconds to 2% of final value
  • Overshoot: < 10% for most missions
  • Steady-State Error: < 0.001° for precision pointing
Disturbance Rejection
% Disturbance to output transfer function
T_dist = P / (1 + P*K);
% Analyze impulse response for disturbance rejection capability

Frequency Domain Analysis

Sensitivity Functions
  • S(jω): Tracking error to reference
  • T(jω): Output to reference (complementary sensitivity)
  • KS(jω): Control effort to reference
Loop Shaping Requirements
% Desired loop shape characteristics
w_gc = 0.1;  % Gain crossover frequency [rad/s]
slope_near_gc = -20;  % dB/decade near crossover
high_freq_rolloff = -40;  % dB/decade at high frequency
Industry Standards
NASA Standards
  • Minimum Gain Margin: 6 dB
  • Minimum Phase Margin: 30°
  • Recommended Margins: 10 dB GM, 45° PM
  • Stability Verification: Required with 20% parameter variations
ESA Standards
  • Stability Margins: 6 dB GM, 30° PM minimum
  • Robustness: Must tolerate ±20% parameter variations
  • Performance: Meet specifications with 3σ dispersion These conservative margins ensure that even with unmodeled dynamics, parameter variations, and nonlinearities, the AOCS remains stable throughout the mission lifetime.

Design Validation Process

Nominal Performance
% Step response analysis
step_info = stepinfo(T_cl);
fprintf('Rise Time: %.2f s\n', step_info.RiseTime);
fprintf('Settling Time: %.2f s\n', step_info.SettlingTime);
fprintf('Overshoot: %.1f%%\n', step_info.Overshoot);
Robustness Analysis
% Monte Carlo analysis with parameter variations
for mc_iter = 1:100
    % Perturb parameters within uncertainty bounds
    J_pert = J_nom .* (1 + 0.2*(2*rand(3,3)-1));
    % Test stability with perturbed model
    [is_stable, margins] = checkStability(perturbed_system);
end
Flexible Mode Excitation
% Test controller response to flexible mode frequencies
for i = 1:length(flexible_freqs)
    t = 0:0.01:10;
    u_excite = 0.1 * sin(2*pi*flexible_freqs(i) * t);
    response = lsim(T_cl, u_excite, t);
    % Check that flexible modes are adequately damped
end

and, I am done writing for the day!

The results of this simple design are below for you to see:

Mission Type: conservative
Controller Order: 8
Stability Margins:
  Gain Margin:  17.53 dB (Required: ≥ 8.0 dB)
  Phase Margin: 81.71° (Required: ≥ 45.0°)
Performance Metrics:
  Rise Time:    20.026 s
  Settling Time: 28.658 s
  Overshoot:    0.00 %
  Peak Torque:  1.4121 Nm

If you’re into Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) for satellites, buckle up: we’re diving deep into how…
items.content_html:
In physics and astrodynamics, this isn’t just some abstract puzzle—it’s an interesting concept for satellite engineers trying to keep their billion-dollar toys from wandering off into the void. If you’re into Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) for satellites, buckle up: we’re diving deep into how this gravitational tango shapes everything from orbit design to keeping your GPS from glitching out. I’ll keep it lively, promise.

A Deeper History: From Newton to Poincaré

Sir Isaac Newton kicked things off in 1687 with his Principia, nailing the two-body problem but admitting the three-body version was a brain-buster. He derived the inverse-square law, leading to:

$$ \ddot{\vec{r}} = -G M \frac{\vec{r}}{r^3} $$

for central force, but adding a third body exploded the complexity. Enter Leonhard Euler in the 1760s, who found the collinear equilibrium points (what we’d call $L_1$, $L_2$, $L_3$) by solving the force balance:

$$ \frac{m_1}{d_1^2} = \frac{m_2}{d_2^2} + \frac{m_1 + m_2}{D^2} $$

where $D$ is the primary separation, $d_1$ and $d_2$ distances. Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 1772 expanded this to equilateral points ($L_4$, $L_5$), proving stability for small mass ratios via perturbation theory.

Fast-forward to Henri Poincaré in the 1880s: Competing for King Oscar II’s prize, he accidentally birthed chaos theory by showing sensitive dependence on initial conditions. His Poincaré sections—plotting phase space intersections—revealed the tangled web of orbits. Unsung hero? Karl Sundman in 1912, who found a series solution converging too slowly for practicality:

$$ \vec{r}(t) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n (t - t_0)^{n/3} $$

Useless for computation but theoretically cool. In the 20th century, numerical methods took over, thanks to computers. For satellites, this history matters because GNC relies on these foundations—Lagrange points for parking, Poincaré for understanding instability.

Entertaining detour: Imagine Newton at a party complaining about headaches, while Poincaré spikes the punch with chaos. By the 1960s, NASA used three-body approximations for Apollo, calculating lunar transfers with patched conics (two-body segments glued together).

The Basics: What Is the Three-Body Problem, Anyway?

Three bodies, mutual gravity—no holds barred. Positions $(\vec{r}_1$, $\vec{r}_2$, $\vec{r}_3)$, masses $(m_1, m_2, m_3)$. The full equations:

$$ m_1 \ddot{\vec{r}}_1 = G m_1 m_2 \frac{\vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1}{|\vec{r}_2 -\vec{r}_1|^3} + G m_1 m_3 \frac{\vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_1}{|\vec{r}_3 - \vec{r}_1|^3} $$

And cyclic for others. Reduce by barycenter frame, but nonlinearity persists. Conserved quantities: total energy $E$,

$ E = \frac{1}{2} \sum m_i \dot{\vec{r}}i^2 - G \sum{i<j} \frac{m_i m_j}{r_{ij}} $

Angular momentum $(\vec{L} = \sum m_i \vec{r}_i \times \dot{\vec{r}}_i)$, linear momentum zero in barycenter.

Special solutions: Euler’s collinear (unstable except for tiny perturbations), Lagrange’s triangular (stable for $(\mu < 1/27)$). For satellites, if one mass is tiny (restricted problem), it simplifies.

Numerical solving? Euler method is basic but error-prone: $(\vec{r}_{n+1} = \vec{r}_n + \Delta t \dot{\vec{r}}_n + \frac{1}{2} \Delta t^2 \ddot{\vec{r}}_n)$.

Prefer Verlet for symplectic preservation: $$(\vec{r}_{n+1} = 2\vec{r}n - \vec{r}{n-1} + \Delta t^2 \ddot{\vec{r}}_n)$$.

In GNC, propagate these for trajectory prediction—e.g., a GPS satellite feels Earth, Moon, Sun tugs, accumulating km-level errors without correction.

Chaos alert: Lyapunov exponents measure divergence. For three-body, positive exponents mean tiny errors balloon exponentially, like butterflies causing space wrecks.

The Restricted Three-Body Problem

For satellites (negligible mass), assume primaries circle. Normalize: distance 1, time so $G(m1+m2)=1, (\mu = m_2/(m_1+m_2))$.

Inertial to rotating frame: Add centrifugal $(\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}))$, Coriolis $(-2\vec{\omega} \times \dot{\vec{r}})$.

Effective potential (\Omega = \frac{1}{2}(x^2 + y^2) + \frac{1-\mu}{r_1} + \frac{\mu}{r_2}).

Derive equations via Lagrangian (L = T - V), with T including Coriolis in generalized coords.

Euler-Lagrange: (\frac{d}{dt} (\partial L / \partial \dot{q}) = \partial L / \partial q).

Yields:

$$ \ddot{x} - 2\dot{y} = \partial \Omega / \partial x = x - (1-\mu)(x+\mu)/r_1^3 - \mu(x-1+\mu)/r_2^3 $$

Similar for y, z (planar if z=0).

Jacobi integral: C = 2\Omega - v^2, constant, defines zero-velocity surfaces—hills blocking motion.

Lagrange points: (\nabla \Omega = 0).

For L1 (collinear, between): Solve quintic $$ (1-\mu)(x+\mu)^2 / |x+\mu|^5 + \mu(x-1+\mu)^2 / |x-1+\mu|^5 = 1 $$.

Approx for small (\mu): x ≈ 1 - (μ/3)^{1/3}.

Stability: Linearize, eigenvalues. For L4/L5, characteristic equation yields stability if (27\mu(1-\mu) < 1).

In GNC, use for halo orbits: Periodic solutions around L-points, solved via differential corrections—guess, integrate, adjust to close.

Sample simulation: Using Python/SciPy, I simulated a 3D halo-ish orbit in Earth-Moon CR3BP ((\mu=0.01215)), initial [x=1.01, y=0, z=0.1, vx=0, vy=-0.1, vz=0]. Over non-dim time 0-10 (about 2-3 months real), x oscillates ~0.97-1.01, y ±0.02, z ±0.1. Key points: At t=0: (1.01,0,0.1); t=5: ~ (0.99, -0.01, 0.08); t=10: ~ (1.00, 0.01, 0.06). This shows the figure-8 in y-z projection, vital for GNC planning.

Perturbations and Extensions: When Three Bodies Aren’t Enough (Solar Wind and Four-Body Fun)

Real satellites face extras: Solar radiation pressure (SRP), oblateness, more bodies. SRP acceleration: $$\vec{a}_{SRP} = -\frac{P A (1+q)}{m c} \hat{s}$$, P solar flux, q reflectivity.

In CR3BP with SRP: Modify potential, shifts L-points sunward.

Four-body: Bi-circular restricted (BCR4BP) adds Sun perturbing Earth-Moon. Equations augment with Sun term.

For Mars-Phobos-Sun (from recent papers), BCR4BP models PNT constellations.

Chaos intensifies: KAM theorem says some tori survive perturbations, but most orbits ergodic.

Satellite GNC: Guidance – Charting Paths Through Gravitational Gauntlets (Optimization Overload)

Guidance plans trajectories. In three-body, use manifolds: Stable/unstable from Floquet multipliers.

TPBVP: Shooting with Newton: $$\mathbf{v}_{k+1} = \mathbf{v}_k - ( \partial \mathbf{r}/\partial \mathbf{v} )^{-1} \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{v}_k)$$.

Optimal control: Pontryagin—minimize fuel via Hamiltonian, bang-bang for impulses.

Example: Low-thrust to L2, solve with indirect methods, costates satisfying adjoint equations.

Recent twist: Generative AI for trajectories (2025 paper)—neural nets approximate solutions faster than numerics.

Navigation: Pinpointing Position Amid Tug-of-War (Filters and Fusion Frenzy)

EKF details: Linearize f, h; Jacobian F = df/dx, predict covariance P = F P F^T + Q.

Third-body perturbation: Series expansion for Moon on GEO: a_p ≈ GM_moon (3 cos^2 ψ -1)/2 d^3, ψ angle.

For Artemis, onboard Kalman fuses ranging, star trackers.

GDOP/PDOP: Geometric factors, PDOP = trace( (H^T H)^{-1} )^{1/2}, H measurement matrix.

In Mars PNT (2025 study), Axial orbits give low PDOP <5 over surface.

Control: Wrestling Orbits into Submission (Feedback Loops and Fuel Frugality)

LQR: Solve algebraic Riccati for infinite horizon: $$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$.

For halos, time-varying LQR on periodic A(t).

Station-keeping: Delta-v ~1-5 m/s/year at L2.

Tethered assists (2024 paper): Optimize ΔC_J = -2ΔΩ + Δv^2 for binary asteroids, genetic algo evolves parameters.

Real-World Examples: Missions Mastering the Mayhem (Classic to Cutting-Edge 2025)

Classics: JWST halo at Sun-Earth L2, fuel savings via manifolds.

Artemis I (2022, but 2025 analysis): ICPS disposal—CR3BP shows mimics Lyapunov/DPO orbits, returns in months if C3 low. Equations as above, stats: 3.5% quick returns, GNC lesson: Avoid geometry corridors.

Hera (launched 2024): Binary asteroid Dimorphos, tethered gravity assists in CR3BP optimize capture, max ΔJacobi for fuel-free orbits.

Mars PNT architectures (2025 prelim): CR3BP/BCR4BP for L3/L4/L5 orbits, constellations of 3 Axials for full coverage, PDOP metrics guide GNC.

OSIRIS-REx: Bennu three-body nav.

Future: Moonshot SPACE 2025 talks CR3BP transports.

Recent Advances: 2024-2025 Breakthroughs in Three-Body Trickery

Artemis disposal paper (2025): EPPR frame refines CR3BP, predicts returns via periodic orbits—key for GNC debris mitigation.

Multi-body Mars (2025): BCR4BP evaluates orbit families for PNT, stability index |λ_max + 1/λ_max| <2 stable.

Tethered assists: Genetic optimization in CR3BP for binaries, relevant to Hera/Janus.

Generative Astrodynamics: AI designs trajectories, solving ODEs via ML surrogates.

Hidden barriers: New surfaces complement zero-velocity, restricting motion further.

Wrapping Up: The Eternal Gravitational Groove (And Why It Rocks)

Doubling the dive, we’ve unpacked equations, simulations, and missions galore. Three-body’s chaos fuels GNC innovation—from Apollo to Artemis returns. Experiment: Code your own CR3BP, tweak initials, watch divergence. Space awaits—keep dancing! 🚀

items.title: Genetic Algorithms for Optimal System Design
items.summary: The inspiration for Genetic Algorithms (GAs) comes directly from nature, specifically from the process of evolution and natural selection. In this article, we discuss how this natural process works in detail and how it inspired the creation of an algorithm for problem-solving and optimization. In…
items.content_html:
The inspiration for Genetic Algorithms (GAs) comes directly from nature, specifically from the process of evolution and natural selection. In this article, we discuss how this natural process works in detail and how it inspired the creation of an algorithm for problem-solving and optimization.

Natural Evolution: Survival of the Fittest

In nature, evolution is the gradual process through which species change over time to adapt to their environment. The main idea behind evolution is natural selection, often called “survival of the fittest.”

Here’s how it works:

  • Genetic Material (DNA): Every living organism has genes, which carry the information that defines its traits—like height, color, or behavior. These genes are stored in a long sequence of DNA.
  • Population: A group of individuals of the same species living together. Each individual has a unique set of genes.
  • Fitness: Not all individuals are equally fit to survive in their environment. Some have traits that help them thrive (like better camouflage or stronger muscles), while others may have traits that make life harder.
  • Reproduction: Individuals that are better suited to their environment (more “fit”) are more likely to survive and reproduce. Their offspring inherit the genes that made their parents successful.
  • Mutation and Variation: Occasionally, random mutations occur in the genes of individuals. These small, random changes introduce new traits. Most mutations might not be useful, but some can provide advantages, helping future generations survive even better.

Example of Evolution in Nature

Let’s take an example of birds living on an island where food is scarce, and only birds with sharp, strong beaks can break open the tough seeds available:

  • Over time, birds with stronger beaks will survive better because they can eat more food.
  • These strong-beaked birds will reproduce more often, passing on the “strong beak” trait to their offspring.
  • Birds with weaker beaks will struggle to survive and may not reproduce as much.
  • Over many generations, more birds in the population will have strong beaks, and the weak-beaked birds will eventually disappear.
  • If a random mutation makes some birds’ beaks even stronger or sharper, that trait could spread if it offers an advantage.

Through this process of selection, reproduction, and mutation, species evolve and adapt to their environments over time.

How This Inspired Genetic Algorithms

The process of evolution in nature inspired scientists to create a problem-solving algorithm that mimics how species evolve to find the “best” solution over time. Here’s how the inspiration from nature was used:

  • Genes and Chromosomes → Variables and Solutions: In a Genetic Algorithm, just like in nature, we represent each possible solution to a problem as a chromosome. This chromosome is made up of genes, each representing different variables or characteristics of the solution.
  • Fitness in Nature → Fitness Function in Algorithms: In nature, fitness is the ability to survive and reproduce. In a Genetic Algorithm, fitness is a measure of how good a solution is at solving the problem.
  • Selection in Nature → Selection in Algorithms: In evolution, only the fittest individuals survive and reproduce. In Genetic Algorithms, the fittest solutions (those with the best fitness scores) are selected to create new solutions.
  • Crossover in Nature → Crossover in Algorithms: In biology, reproduction combines the genes from two parents to create offspring with a mix of both parents’ traits. In Genetic Algorithms, crossover mimics this by combining two solutions to create a new one.
  • Mutation in Nature → Mutation in Algorithms: Just as random mutations in nature can introduce new traits, mutation in a Genetic Algorithm introduces small random changes to a solution. This ensures that new possibilities are explored, even if the current best solutions aren’t perfect.

The key advantage of using evolution as inspiration is that Genetic Algorithms don’t need to know the exact path to the best solution. They explore many possibilities and use random variation, just like evolution, to stumble upon better solutions. Over time, by applying selection and combination, these algorithms can find highly effective solutions to very complex problems.

Example: Maximizing a Non-Linear Function

Let’s say we want to maximize the function:

[ f(x) = x \sin(10\pi x) + 2 ]

where ( x ) is between 0 and 1. This is a non-linear function with multiple peaks and valleys, making it a good example for a Genetic Algorithm. The MATLAB code below demonstrates this optimization.

% Compare Genetic Algorithm solution with standard mathematical optimization
% for maximizing f(x) = x * sin(10 * pi * x) + 2, x in [0, 1]

% Step 1: Genetic Algorithm solution
% Genetic Algorithm parameters
populationSize = 20;  % Number of individuals in the population
numGenerations = 50;  % Number of generations to evolve
mutationRate = 0.05;  % Probability of mutation (5%)
crossoverRate = 0.8;  % Probability of crossover (80%)
numGenes = 16;        % Number of genes (bits) to represent x

% Create an initial random population
population = randi([0 1], populationSize, numGenes);

% Genetic Algorithm loop for several generations
for generation = 1:numGenerations
    % Evaluate the fitness of each individual in the population
    fitness = zeros(populationSize, 1);
    for i = 1:populationSize
        x = decodeChromosome(population(i, :), numGenes);
        fitness(i) = fitnessFunction(x);
    end
    
    % Select individuals for reproduction based on fitness
    selectedPopulation = selectRoulette(population, fitness);
    
    % Apply crossover to generate a new population
    newPopulation = applyCrossover(selectedPopulation, crossoverRate);
    
    % Apply mutation to introduce random changes
    mutatedPopulation = applyMutation(newPopulation, mutationRate);
    
    % Update the population for the next generation
    population = mutatedPopulation;
end

% Final solution from Genetic Algorithm
[bestFitness_GA, bestIndex] = max(fitness);
bestX_GA = decodeChromosome(population(bestIndex, :), numGenes);
fprintf('Genetic Algorithm: Best x = %.5f, Best fitness = %.5f\n', bestX_GA, bestFitness_GA);

% Step 2: Standard Mathematical Optimization using fminbnd
% fminbnd minimizes a function, so we minimize -f(x) to maximize f(x)
objectiveFunction = @(x) -(x * sin(10 * pi * x) + 2);  % Negative of the function to maximize
[x_fminbnd, neg_fminbnd] = fminbnd(objectiveFunction, 0, 1);

% Convert the negative fitness value back to positive
bestFitness_fminbnd = -neg_fminbnd;

fprintf('Standard Optimization (fminbnd): Best x = %.5f, Best fitness = %.5f\n', x_fminbnd, bestFitness_fminbnd);

%% Function to evaluate the fitness of a chromosome
function fit = fitnessFunction(x)
    % The function we want to maximize: f(x) = x * sin(10 * pi * x) + 2
    fit = x * sin(10 * pi * x) + 2;
end

%% Function to decode the chromosome (binary string) into a real value of x
function x = decodeChromosome(chromosome, numGenes)
    % Convert binary chromosome to decimal, then scale to the range [0, 1]
    decimalValue = bin2dec(num2str(chromosome));
    x = decimalValue / (2^numGenes - 1);  % Scale to [0, 1]
end

%% Function for roulette wheel selection based on fitness
function selectedPop = selectRoulette(population, fitness)
    % Normalize fitness values to create a probability distribution
    totalFitness = sum(fitness);
    prob = fitness / totalFitness;
    
    % Cumulative probability distribution for selection
    cumulativeProb = cumsum(prob);
    
    % Select individuals based on roulette wheel approach
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    selectedPop = zeros(populationSize, numGenes);
    for i = 1:populationSize
        r = rand();  % Random number between 0 and 1
        selectedIndex = find(cumulativeProb >= r, 1);  % Select individual
        selectedPop(i, :) = population(selectedIndex, :);
    end
end

%% Function to apply crossover between pairs of individuals
function newPop = applyCrossover(population, crossoverRate)
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    newPop = population;
    
    for i = 1:2:populationSize
        if rand() < crossoverRate
            % Select two parents
            parent1 = population(i, :);
            parent2 = population(i+1, :);
            
            % Apply crossover (single-point crossover)
            crossoverPoint = randi([1, numGenes-1]);
            newPop(i, :) = [parent1(1:crossoverPoint), parent2(crossoverPoint+1:end)];
            newPop(i+1, :) = [parent2(1:crossoverPoint), parent1(crossoverPoint+1:end)];
        end
    end
end

%% Function to apply mutation to the population
function mutatedPop = applyMutation(population, mutationRate)
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    mutatedPop = population;
    
    for i = 1:populationSize
        for j = 1:numGenes
            if rand() < mutationRate
                mutatedPop(i, j) = ~population(i, j);  % Flip the gene
            end
        end
    end
end

Upon evaluating this code in MATLAB, we get the following output:

Genetic Algorithm: Best x = 0.59976, Best fitness = 2.84666
Standard Optimization (fminbnd): Best x = 0.65156, Best fitness = 2.65078

Example: Satellite Trajectory Optimization

Let’s consider an example related to satellite systems. To compare a Genetic Algorithm (GA) with a standard optimization technique for trajectory optimization of a satellite in orbit, we address the following problem:

Problem Description

The goal is to transfer a satellite from one circular orbit (initial orbit) to another circular orbit (target orbit). The satellite can change its velocity at specific times (impulse burns), and we want to optimize the thrust directions to minimize the total fuel used.

Objective: Minimize fuel usage (which is proportional to the velocity changes (\Delta V)).

Standard Method for Comparison: We can use the Hohmann transfer method, which is an analytically optimal way of transferring a satellite between two circular orbits using two impulsive burns.

Steps

  • Use a Genetic Algorithm to find the optimal thrust direction and times to minimize (\Delta V).
  • Compare it with the fuel cost of a Hohmann transfer (a standard two-burn maneuver).

Problem Setup

  • The satellite is initially in a circular orbit with radius ( r_1 ).
  • The target orbit is circular with radius ( r_2 ).
  • The satellite can apply a finite number of thrust impulses at any point during the transfer.
  • The cost function is the total (\Delta V) (change in velocity).

MATLAB Code

% Constants
mu = 398600;  % Gravitational parameter of Earth [km^3/s^2]
r1 = 7000;    % Initial orbit radius [km]
r2 = 10000;   % Target orbit radius [km]

% Genetic Algorithm parameters
populationSize = 50;  % Number of individuals in the population
numGenerations = 100; % Number of generations
numSteps = 2;         % Number of impulsive burns (same as Hohmann transfer)
mutationRate = 0.05;  % Mutation probability
crossoverRate = 0.8;  % Crossover probability

% Initial random population (representing thrust directions)
population = rand(populationSize, numSteps * 2);  % Thrust angles and magnitudes

% Preallocate fitness array for storing results
fitness = zeros(populationSize, 1);

% GA loop for multiple generations
for generation = 1:numGenerations
    % Evaluate fitness (fuel consumption) of each individual
    for i = 1:populationSize
        thrustSequence = population(i, :);
        fitness(i) = evaluateThrustSequence(thrustSequence, r1, r2, mu);
    end
    
    % Select individuals based on fitness (lower deltaV is better)
    selectedPopulation = selectRoulette(population, fitness);
    
    % Apply crossover to generate new population
    newPopulation = applyCrossover(selectedPopulation, crossoverRate);
    
    % Apply mutation to introduce diversity
    mutatedPopulation = applyMutation(newPopulation, mutationRate);
    
    % Update the population
    population = mutatedPopulation;
    
    % Display best fitness of the generation
    [bestFitness, bestIndex] = min(fitness);  % We minimize fuel consumption
    fprintf('Generation %d: Best deltaV = %.5f km/s\n', generation, bestFitness);
end

% Final GA solution
[bestFitness, bestIndex] = min(fitness);
bestThrustSequence = population(bestIndex, :);
fprintf('Optimal deltaV found by GA: %.5f km/s\n', bestFitness);

% Hohmann transfer calculations
% Burn 1: At perigee (first circular orbit)
v1 = sqrt(mu / r1);  % Velocity in initial orbit
a_transfer = (r1 + r2) / 2;  % Semi-major axis of the transfer orbit
v_transfer_perigee = sqrt(2 * mu * (1/r1 - 1/(2 * a_transfer)));  % Velocity at perigee of transfer orbit

% Burn 2: At apogee (target circular orbit)
v_transfer_apogee = sqrt(2 * mu * (1/r2 - 1/(2 * a_transfer)));  % Velocity at apogee of transfer orbit
v2 = sqrt(mu / r2);  % Velocity in target orbit

% Delta V for Hohmann transfer
deltaV_hohmann = abs(v_transfer_perigee - v1) + abs(v2 - v_transfer_apogee);
fprintf('DeltaV for Hohmann transfer: %.5f km/s\n', deltaV_hohmann);

%% Function to evaluate fuel usage for a given thrust sequence
function deltaV = evaluateThrustSequence(thrustSequence, r1, r2, mu)
    % Simplified evaluation of fuel consumption for impulsive burns
    % thrustSequence: contains magnitudes and angles of thrust
    
    % Step 1: Calculate initial velocity in the initial orbit
    v1 = sqrt(mu / r1);  % Circular orbit velocity at r1
    
    % Step 2: Compute the semi-major axis of the transfer orbit
    a_transfer = (r1 + r2) / 2;  % Semi-major axis of the transfer orbit
    
    % Step 3: Compute velocity at perigee (initial point) and apogee
    v_transfer_perigee = sqrt(2 * mu * (1/r1 - 1/(2 * a_transfer)));  % Perigee velocity
    v_transfer_apogee = sqrt(2 * mu * (1/r2 - 1/(2 * a_transfer)));    % Apogee velocity
    
    % Step 4: Calculate deltaV for each thrust impulse
    deltaV1 = abs(v_transfer_perigee - v1);   % First burn at perigee (r1)
    deltaV2 = abs(v_transfer_apogee - sqrt(mu / r2));  % Second burn at apogee (r2)
    
    % Total deltaV (fuel consumption)
    deltaV = deltaV1 + deltaV2;
end

%% Function for roulette wheel selection based on fitness
function selectedPop = selectRoulette(population, fitness)
    totalFitness = sum(fitness);
    prob = fitness / totalFitness;
    cumulativeProb = cumsum(prob);
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    selectedPop = zeros(populationSize, numGenes);
    
    for i = 1:populationSize
        r = rand();
        selectedIndex = find(cumulativeProb >= r, 1);
        selectedPop(i, :) = population(selectedIndex, :);
    end
end

%% Function to apply crossover
function newPop = applyCrossover(population, crossoverRate)
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    newPop = population;
    
    for i = 1:2:populationSize
        if rand() < crossoverRate
            parent1 = population(i, :);
            parent2 = population(i+1, :);
            crossoverPoint = randi([1, numGenes-1]);
            newPop(i, :) = [parent1(1:crossoverPoint), parent2(crossoverPoint+1:end)];
            newPop(i+1, :) = [parent2(1:crossoverPoint), parent1(crossoverPoint+1:end)];
        end
    end
end

%% Function to apply mutation
function mutatedPop = applyMutation(population, mutationRate)
    populationSize = size(population, 1);
    numGenes = size(population, 2);
    mutatedPop = population;
    
    for i = 1:populationSize
        for j = 1:numGenes
            if rand() < mutationRate
                mutatedPop(i, j) = rand();  % Random mutation
            end
        end
    end
end

Both methods yield a (\Delta V) value of 1.22288 km/s. This is a simplified illustrative example, and Genetic Algorithms are particularly useful for more complex optimization scenarios.

Conclusion

If you have any questions related to this topic, feel free to connect with me and message me at https://www.linkedin.com/in/yajur.

Keep Exploring!
